data.title: ServiceTitan, Inc. 2026 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by ServiceTitan, Inc. in conjunction with their 2026 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: THOR Industries: Accelerating With Caution After The Potholes
data.description:
THOR Industries, Inc. (NYSE:THO) has experienced softer market conditions in the past two years. In my previous coverage, I already discussed how it had to endure a tough FY23 and FY24. On a lighter note, it exuded resilience through cost and expense management to stay profitable and sustainable.

At this point, we are slowly seeing the fruit of its labor with its notable improvement in the most recent quarter. The RV market remains quite weaker than its 2021-2022 high, but its trajectory is a positive one. By the looks of it, RV companies like THO may be on their way to recovery. However, they may still watch out for macroeconomic risks today.

With respect to the price, the stock appears more fairly valued now than in my previous coverage. Technicals are weak, but buying opportunities are easier to find.

THO Q3 2025: A Gradual Recovery

After quarters of weak performances, THOR Industries, Inc. has finally regained its strength to prove its durability, Its Q3 2025 operating revenue amounted to $2.9B, up by 3.3% YoY from $2.8B. It was also 43% higher than in Q2 2025. While we can attribute this large increase to seasonality, its improvement was visible. From Q1 2024 to Q2 2025, THO experienced a YoY revenue decrease. After six quarters, THO was able to enjoy revenue growth once again.

North American Towable RVs remained its primary growth driver, with a 9.1% YoY increase. Its shipments also increased by 5.5% or 1,883 units, showing increased demand. This was also the segment where THO really had true recovery. Aside from a higher sales volume, THO enjoyed an increase in the average price from $31,334 to $32,400. In addition, its gross profit margin increased by 2.0 bps. For its North American Motorized RVs, THO drove demand using its pricing strategy. Shipments rose by 10.9% or 543 units, while the average price dropped from $130,327 to $121,062. Nonetheless, it still worked, as sales rose by 3.1% YoY.

Overall, I think what really drove THO was prudent product diversification and inventory management. Its increased focus on Towable RVs allows it to capitalize on what it does best. It is in line with the market demand, so focusing on it also helps it improve its sales and margins. I will discuss this more later. The revenue increase in this segment completely offsets the weakness in its European RV segment.

This move also helped it improve its cost and expense management. As a result, its operating leverage slightly weakened from 90.2% to 90.0%, showing its well-managed cost sensitivity. Its gross profit margin improved from 15.1% to 15.3%. Lastly, its net income margin rose from 4.1% to 4.6%. If we make the income tax expenses the same as the previous year, it will decrease to 4.4%. This will still be higher, showing improved profitability.

New Tariffs Versus Lower Oil Prices

These are external aspects that may influence macroeconomic conditions and THO's performance.

Higher tariffs can raise THO's cost pressures and affect margins in the long run. Why? Increasing tariffs also increase the cost of importing auto parts and components. Retaliation may also take place. So, without proper implementation and plans, THO may have a lower pricing flexibility to cover extra expenses.

On a lighter note, I think lower oil prices can partially offset tariffs. For instance, WTI and Brent move at the 52-week low. This downtrend may be sustained by the OPEC supply hike this July. Considering this, oil prices may stay below $70 per barrel. As a result, shipping and transportation may benefit from these positive spillovers. In addition, RV travel may have renewed demand as lower oil prices may also lower fuel expenses. This can help American travelers save more money by travelling domestically instead of by plane. In a recent survey, 44M of Americans will do RV travel this summer.

RV Recovery

The RV industry is recovering from its weak condition in 2023-2024. Pricing remains a key driver, especially for motorhomes, since these are more expensive. We saw this in my computation of the average price per unit of THO's motorhome RVs. That is why we saw a slight improvement in motorhome shipments last March. It was the first time that motorhome shipments had a positive YoY change. In April, it weakened again by -3.4%. Yet, it was better than in previous months, mostly a double-digit YoY decrease. Meanwhile, Towable RVs are the crown jewel of THO and the whole industry. Total shipments rose by 4.2% YoY to 31,982 units. Hence, total shipments still increased by 3.4% YoY from 34,197 to 35,375 RVs.

This positive may be sustained in the second half as summer trips highlight the popularity of RVing. This summer, 42% of all planned trips will be road trips. What's even more interesting is that RV's popularity has already been passed down from generation to generation. Millennials are taking the lead, with a 40% probability of purchasing RVs in 2026. Meanwhile, Gen Zers topped the list of online searches for RVs with 45% of the total number.

Another potential catalyst is the increased preference for remote work and learning. So, parents and their children can travel together while working and studying. In addition, travelling by RV can be a cheaper option than international trips or beaches. A survey shows that over 60% of Americans won't travel this summer because they can't afford it. Among those who will travel, the majority choose domestic trips.

Liquidity Remains Excellent

Aside from these market trends, THO capitalizes on its strong liquidity to ensure sustainability. As we can see, its cash has increased considerably in the past 11 quarters to $508M. One factor to consider is its increased profitability, driven by its strategic cost and expense management. If we look at its cash flow statement, THO has maintained a positive FCF in the past five quarters. In Q3 2025, it generated an FCF of $224M, which means that it converted 8% of its sales into free cash. Another crucial aspect is its effective inventory management. THO carefully produced RVs corresponding to the market demand. The decreasing trend of inventory shows three things. First, it produced less to balance its demand and supply. Second, it produced less to manage costs and expenses better. Third, demand is starting to recover.

More interestingly, its borrowings have considerably decreased. This shows that RVs has been generating enough cash inflows and/or earnings to repay its borrowings. In fact, its cash can cover half of its borrowings in a single payment. With its Net Debt/EBITDA of 0.84x, THO only needs about 10 months to cover all borrowings at once. This means that it has enough time and resources to prepare for its repayments.

Valuation

THO has already dropped by -10.4% since my previous coverage, in line with my hold rating. At its current price, THO appears more reasonable and fairly valued. We can assess its valuation relative to key metrics, such as BVPS and Sales Per Share or SPS. If we use the P/B, the price is now trading at 1.37x its BVPS, which is cheaper than the five-year average of 1.54x. This suggests a TP of $96.93, a 13% upside potential. A similar trend can be seen in its P/S Ratio, although it leads to a lower TP of $88.50, a 3% upside potential.

Moreover, the price of THO is already more realistic if we check the DCF Model I used in my previous coverage. With my TP of $90.43, the model gives a 5% upside. Hence, buying at its current price is already reasonable.

Technicals

THO has shown improvements in its momentum since its correction. While its bullish signals remain weak, we can see its continued recovery. The price keeps increasing, while the 50-day SMA has already flattened, showing a potential shift in market sentiments. Yet, we may need more confirmation to confirm bullish signals. One indication is when the price crosses above the 200-day SMA. Another is when the 50-day SMA moves up again.

For MACD and RSI, we cannot detect any bullish or bearish divergence yet. THO has been consolidating in the past three weeks. The sudden price jump yesterday was due to the Q3 2025 results, but the trading day ended with sellers still pulling the price down from $89-91 to $84-86.

It is also worth noting that before the Q3 release, the consolidation happens slightly below the 0.382 Fibonacci Retracement, which suggests testing of the first resistance at $82, which has already been broken a few times. So, the price jump could be a normal reaction as buyers tried to push the price up to the 0.50 retracement point before decreasing again. Considering this trend, the price may retrace to $83-84 again as momentum improves. It is also interesting to note that the 0.50 retracement point is also very close to my DCF TP. Hence, the current price is already a good entry point.

Conclusion

THOR Industries, Inc. remains a solid company amid the gradual market recovery. Its prudent product mix and inventory management allow it to stay profitable and enjoy strong cash flows. It is also fairly valued today, with a decent upside potential. In addition, technicals show some buying opportunities, making the price an ideal entry point. I am upgrading my rating on this stock from hold to buy.

data.title: Broadcom's Post-Q2 Earnings Pullback Is A Classic Overreaction
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) reported 2QFY25; sales grew 20% Y/Y to $15B, more or less in line with estimates of $14.97B, and guidance for 5% sequential growth next quarter to $15.8B in sales was once again in line with estimates. In line is basically a miss in terms of market reaction; so, AVGO stock is down almost 5% in extended trading on guidance not surpassing estimates materially. Heading into print, the stock was on a run; in fact, it was the best-performing semi name in our coverage since 2QCY25 began, up 55% since the end of March and almost 30% on the 1M chart. So, while AVGO continues to dominate the AI custom ASIC business and outperforms expectations with its networking ICs pushing up AI revenues, the stock still dropped. This is what we like to call an illogical sell-off, or a gift. We're upgrading Broadcom to a strong buy on what is shaping up to be a post-earnings sell off and reset expectations.

The above showcases AVGO stock movement post-earnings in extended trading and the stock run-up since we last said buy in early April on the Investing Experts Podcast with Rena, since which the stock is up 66% versus the S&P 500 up 19%. We believe the post-earnings sell off Friday will be a rare window to add this stock.

Pay Attention to What Matters

For the quarter, AVGO reported semi sales up 2.4% Q/Q and 17% Y/Y to $8.41B supported by their AI-related demand and saw AI revenues specifically grow 7% Q/Q to $4.4B. Non-AI semi sales were weaker sequentially, down 2% to $4B. This isn't a big scare, however, because it's just the result of seasonal weakness from Apple (AAPL), which, let's not forget, makes up roughly 20% of AVGO's total sales. On the other end, infrastructure software sales were down 1.6% Q/Q to $6.6B. That's the quick overview.

What investors should pay attention to here, in our opinion, is the fact that AI revenues surged 46% Y/Y and that wasn't on ASIC alone, it was on AI networking demand, which, we believe, is AVGO's weapon along with its software side of the business. Why are these Broadcom's weapons? Because they'll allow the company to scale its ASIC business without squeezing margins. AI custom ASIC is a low-margin business; just last month, AVGO's close competitor in the ASIC business, Marvell (MRVL), said on their earnings call that

...within custom, there is a range, and it's like the old adage in semiconductors. The higher the volume, you're going to see lower gross margins.

And while MRVL's gross margins have been on decline for the past five consecutive quarters, AVGO's margins actually expanded. For the quarter, AVGO's non-GAAP gross margins expanded 30 basis points to 79.4%. AVGO's infrastructure software business and its AI networking offset the lower margin ASIC business. And, we expect margins to continue to expand with management guiding for infrastructure software sales to grow to $6.7B next quarter from $6.6B this quarter and for AI revenue to grow 16% Q/Q to $5.1B due to continued networking demand filling through.

What about ASIC? In our opinion, AVGO will easily be the industry's go-to for tier 1 hyperscalers; we're talking Google (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT). While MRVL also has some of these on its customer list, AVGO is the more reliable pick between the two because it has economies of scale and resources. Either way, we think tier 1 players won't stick to being single source, but we see AVGO benefiting most from the ASIC peers. The company now has three main customers: Google, Meta and, supposedly, ByteDance, TikTok's parent company, while overall, it has announced five customers and hinted at two new prospects to come. Even just looking at the top three, AVGO appears to be in good hands, considering these are big players. Google is doubling down on spending $75B in CapEx for the year, and Meta just raised CapEx this quarter from ~$60-65B to $64B to $72B. AVGO designed next-gen TPUv7 3nm for Google and is working on TPUv8 2nm and co-designed MTIA AI chips for Meta and is sampling next-gen 3nm MTIA chip. The chart below outlines a bit of Broadcom's competitive pipeline versus MRVL, and it also provides ramp timing, which shows AVGO has a longer roadmap secured than MRVL.

Management isn't hiding its cards, which is why the stock has run up so much and why AVGO saw 34 upward revenue revisions over the past three months and only three downward ones. All these factors made the risk-reward less attractive heading into print but all the more attractive now. We're expecting demand for ASIC to expand further into next year because mid and low complexity AI workloads will begin to be offloaded from AI GPUs onto ASIC. The reason is that tier 1 guys need to reduce their reliance on Nvidia (NVDA) and lower costs by making a longer-term in-house solution for inference workloads. This is done through ASIC. Although we probably won't see ASIC make a dent in demand for NVDA AI GPUs in the near to mid-term, it'll be the future for inference and one in which AVGO is well positioned. We think the stock falling on this quarter's report makes for a good reset of expectations post-rally and another window to add.

Valuation

AVGO is pricey, and after this quarter's sales and guidance for Q3 are coming in line, investors are obviously worried about whether it's worth the price. We strongly think it is. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 58.35x compared to a group average of 28.38x, and on an EV/Sales basis, the stock trades at 20.25x versus an average of 2.82x. The chart below shows the full scoop of the financials.

Bottom Line: Limited Opportunity to Add Post-reset

While the stock is expensive, we think the premium multiples are there for a reason: this is a growth stock. AVGO's revenue growth is 40% compared to a sector average of 6%, with a forward EPS growth projection of 23% compared to a sector median of 10%. Profitability is also well secured, with an EBITDA margin of 77% versus the sector's average of -3%. AVGO is an AI name and one of the few ones that is getting an expectation reset at the moment, and its AI revenue expectations are manageable; we see a longer-term opportunity for investors to buy the panic. We know we've been waiting for an opportunity like this for AVGO because the stock was on an upward trajectory and within overbought territory since the 9th of May.

data.title: Is It Ethereum's Time Again? ETH And ETH/BTC Technical Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Ethereum (ETH-USD) had a rough beginning of 2025 - failing to reach new all-time highs, while Bitcoin (BTC-USD) smashed through its own ATH multiple times. Solana (SOL-USD), providing a cheaper alternative to ETH services, also held stronger than the Ether throughout the latter part of 2024 and beginning 2025.

ETH performance is key to the overall crypto market performance; the past Altcoin cycles have always been led by ETH performance over BTC.

ETH/BTC is a great cryptocurrency spread for a crypto trader to spot relative performance, provide a direction for which crypto to choose, and track the appetite for altcoins.

Through this crypto market update, you will see how ETH/BTC rising helped altcoin bull runs in the past cycle, something that many crypto traders have been awaiting and is yet to materialize again.

ETH/BTC and its Correlation to total Market Performance

This 2017-2021 chart of ETH/BTC contains essential information about understanding the crypto market.

Through 1 and 2, we observe how the first bull run in ETH and ETH/BTC led to the 2017 total crypto market bull run - taking the market cap from $20-30B average to highs of $620B in November 2017.

Number 3 on the chart shows how a drop in ETH/BTC leads to a significant cooldown of the crypto market, going back towards a total market cap between $100 and $250B from 2018 to the end of 2020.

From end-2020 to the end of 2021, number 4 shows the same correlation of ETH/BTC and crypto markets rallying.

This Covid-era bull run introduced crypto for an ever-bigger number of investors, and led to the flurry of altcoins and cryptocurrency projects such as Doge, Avax, Solana. The crypto market cap went from $300B to $2.86T, and this level only got reached again in November 2024.

Finally, number 5 shows similarly to number 3 how the drop in market cap correlates with a drop in the ETH/BTC spread.

ETH/BTC from 2020 to Today

We take a closer look at the 2021 Bull Run and how the December 2021 top in ETH/BTC led again a significant correction in the crypto market.

2023 was all about Bitcoin Dominance as its rally from $14,475 to its record highs of $112,030 left Ethereum and all other altcoins lagging.

We are seeing a breakout from the descent that started at the same level as the 2020 ETH/BTC bullish breakout - we will see if the spread continues upward and if it generates another bull run for altcoins.

ETH Daily Chart

ETH had a consequent rally after a significant drop between $4,000 highs in December 2024 to $1,363 lows in April 2025.

Prices are consolidating between $2,300 and $2,600. The key is to see if Bitcoin prices that are also stagnating above the $100,000 Mark leads to rallies in other cryptos as the same phenomenon happened in past bull cycles.

Levels to watch:

Support Zones:

  • S1: 2,385 to 2,525
  • S2: 2,035 to 2,167
  • S3: 1,700 to 1,825

Resistance Zones:

  • R1: 2,600 to 2,750
  • R2: 3,225 to 3,363
  • R3: 3,660 to 3,800

ETH 4H Chart

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Mairs & Power Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview | First Quarter 2025

In the first quarter of 2025, the S&P 500 Total Return (TR) was -4.27%, the Russell 2000 (TR) was -9.48%, and the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index was 2.70%.

The year began positively for the stock market, reaching an all-time high in February, but tariff uncertainty in March caused a decline in equities while fixed income benefited from a flight to perceived safety.

Despite the headline indices declining, a deeper look at sector performance of the S&P 500 reveals significant variation in first quarter performance. Growth stocks, particularly in the Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology sectors, saw the sharpest declines, as their valuation levels retreated after a long stretch of outperformance. In response, traditionally “defensive” stocks, namely in Health Care, Consumer Staples, and Utilities, led the market.

While the stock market experienced disruption, the economy continued to show some resilience. The labor market remains healthy, adding 228,000 jobs in March, and the unemployment rate is still low at 4.2%. Additionally, consumer spending and housing market activity improved in recent months, after lulls to begin the year. Manufacturing also showed signs of picking up, with the ISM survey finally rising above 50, indicating expansion. Although this data is now “old news,” it paints a picture of an economy that entered the current upheaval on somewhat stable footing.

However, prospects of a prolonged trade war with China raise concerns about reigniting inflation and slowing economic growth. The uncertainty created by this backdrop clouds decision-making, which is already flashing warning signs with declining consumer and business confidence. The University of Michigan’s Index of Consumer Sentiment fell in March to its lowest level since 2022, and business confidence surveys also weakened.

When faced with prolonged uncertainty, businesses and consumers become more cautious, delaying spending, investment, and hiring decisions. This can lead to a slowdown in economic activity, as decisions to commit resources are paused. The Atlanta Federal Reserve’s GDPNow model, which provides a real-time gauge of economic activity, deteriorated sharply in March, signaling a possible contraction in first quarter GDP.

The potential impact on inflation from tariffs is another cause for concern. While inflation has receded from pandemic highs, it has proven stubborn at 2.8% and is a key source of consumer frustration. At its core, tariffs are a tax, one that either businesses must absorb (at the expense of lower margins), or consumers must absorb by paying higher prices (leading to lower consumption). We expect many businesses to pass on most of this tax to consumers, which would likely create additional near-term upward pressure on prices and inflation. This dynamic of slowing growth and rising inflation (stagflation) puts the Federal Reserve in a difficult position, casting more uncertainty on future interest rate decisions.

Future Outlook

Given the complexity of global supply chains and unknown future responses, we cannot predict what is next, but we know it has created a fog-like uncertainty pervading the economic outlook. Uncertainty is a fact of life, but the current environment certainly creates a more challenging landscape for policymakers and businesses to navigate. The longer uncertainty persists, the harder it is for companies and consumers to make long-term decisions, which ultimately leads to slower economic growth. We expect more volatility in the months ahead as policies and their impact become clearer.

As economic concerns mount, the outlook for corporate earnings is looking more subdued. Current expectations for first quarter S&P 500 profits point to 7.3% growth, but that is down from the 12% growth that was expected at the beginning of the year. A similar trend is occurring with full-year 2025 estimates, which have declined from 12.7% growth to 10.6% growth. Although earnings are still expected to grow this year, estimates are moving lower, and the sharp pullback in stock prices suggests investors are becoming increasingly nervous about the outlook.

At the same time, valuation levels are also contracting, reflecting the murkier outlook for the market. During the first quarter, the S&P 500 forward 12-month price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio fell from 22 times to just over 20 times, in line with the 5-year average though still above the 10-year average of 18.3. Meanwhile, the S&P Small Cap 600 is trading at 14.3 times earnings, a 30% discount to the S&P 500, and well below its 10-year average of 16.7. The recent correction has removed some of the froth from the market, particularly for the large cap growth stocks, with the S&P Equal Weighted P/E now back to its 10-year average of 16.5 times earnings.

Having a sound investment philosophy is always important, but even more so when market conditions become challenging. Rather than investing in markets, our approach of investing in companies with durable business models, healthy balance sheets, and reasonable valuations lends itself to the current environment. We remain committed to this philosophy and will do what we’ve always done, looking for opportunities that we believe will benefit our clients over the long term.

Performance Review

The Mairs & Power Growth Fund was down 4.69% in the first quarter of 2025, slightly underperforming the Fund’s peer group as measured by the Morningstar Large Blend index that declined 3.79%, but in line with the S&P 500 Total Return (TR) index that was down 4.27%.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance of the Fund may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. For the most recent month-end performance figures, please call Shareholder Services at (800) 304-7404. Expense Ratio 0.64%.

The postpandemic bull market came to a screeching halt as the current presidential administration announced sweeping new tariffs that have rattled the markets and stoked fears of higher inflation and recession. In addition, the emergence of DeepSeek, a Chinese artificial intelligence (AI) startup, jolted the Information Technology sector. The company unveiled large language models (LLM) with comparable, if not superior, functionality to that of American rivals at a fraction of the cost. While controversial, DeepSeek’s claims undermine the prevailing wisdom that AI requires exorbitant levels of computing power and called into question the vast amounts of AI investments being made. As such, many of the same stocks that drove market returns over the past few years, the so-called “Magnificent Seven” (Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta, Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA)), abruptly reversed course and dragged down the market with them.

Stock selection was a slight headwind in the quarter as many of our AI-related stocks such as Microsoft (MSFT), nVent (NVT), and NVIDIA (NVDA) pulled back on the DeepSeek news. As we have highlighted in prior quarters, we remain excited about the prospects of AI but we’re less enthusiastic about their lofty valuations and have been reducing our positions accordingly.

Still, we retain large holdings in the companies, and consequently, stock selection suffered.

DeepSeek’s initial claims woefully underestimated the true cost of developing its LLMs yet showcased the natural progression of efficiency improvements that are inherent with technological advancements. As new technologies do, AI will become cheaper, which should lead to greater accessibility and adoption over time.

Given the deterioration in economic conditions, it’s unsurprising that the more defensive sectors, such as Health Care and Utilities, held up relatively well. This helped relative performance since the Fund is overweight in both sectors. The Financials sector was also a standout in the quarter and many of our large financial holdings, including JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Fiserv (FI), and Visa (V), continue to execute extremely well in a difficult operating environment. While we believe these companies’ long-term prospects remain positive, their valuations appear stretched, and we have trimmed our positions in response. Energy was the best performing sector in the quarter due largely to higher natural gas prices as record low temperatures hit large swaths of the United States. The Fund’s lack of Energy exposure was a significant drag on relative performance but not enough to overcome the positive contributions from the aforementioned sectors. As a result, sector selection was a positive contributor to performance.

During the quarter, the Growth Fund initiated a position in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM, OTC:TSMWF) (TSMC). TSMC is the largest semiconductor manufacturing company in the world with over 60% market share of advanced logic chips. In every semiconductor node advancement, transistors are shrunk so they can be packed more densely onto a chip. The smaller the transistor, the more expensive and challenging the manufacturing process becomes. Over the years, TSMC gained market share while competitors fell behind and many exited the market altogether. Almost 70% of TSMC’s revenue comes from the U.S., with Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Intel (INTC) as its two remaining competitors, and each is facing its own unique challenges. We believe that TSMC’s cost discipline and relentless focus on research & development, as well as customer service, positions it well for further share gains. The stock has been hit hard due to the pullback in the Technology sector, and its valuation has become too attractive to pass up.

Tariffs remain the watchword of the day and have introduced a high level of volatility in the markets. In the short term, stock selection may suffer as the market adjusts to the ever-changing policies of the current administration. It’s important to remember that volatility brings opportunities and we’ve been adding to positions and initiating new positions in companies we have been following for years, patiently waiting for attractive entry points. We remain committed to our long-term focus and trust the management teams of our holdings to focus on what is in their power to control, namely innovation and execution, and emerge from the present instability in a better competitive position.

The price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio measures a company’s current share price relative to its per-share earnings.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results.

1 Performance information shown includes the reinvestment of dividend and capital gain distributions, but does not reflect the deduction of taxes that a shareholder would pay on Fund distributions or the redemption of Fund shares.

2 S&P 500 TR Index is an unmanaged index of 500 common stocks that is generally considered representative of the U.S. stock market.

3 Morningstar large-blend portfolio are fairly representative of the overall U.S. stock marketing in size, growth rates, and price. Stocks in the to 70% of the capitalization of the U.S equity market are defined as large-cap. The blend style is assigned to portfolios where neither growth nor value characteristics predominate. These portfolios tend to invest across the spectrum of U.S. industries, and owing to their broad exposure, the portfolios’ returns are often similar to those of the S&P 500 Index.

S&P Small Cap 600 TR Index is an index of small-company stocks managed by Standard and Poor’s that covers a broad range of small cap stocks in the

U.S. The index is weighted according to market capitalization and covers 3-4% of the total market for equities in the U.S. It tracks both the capital gains of a group of stocks over time and assumes that any cash distributions, such as dividends, are reinvested back in the index.

Russell 2000 TR Index is a small-cap stock market index of the smallest 2,000 stocks in the Russell 3000 Index.

Bloomberg Government/Credit Bond Index is a broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the non-securitized component of the U.S. Aggregate Index. It includes investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities.

The ISM manufacturing index, also known as the purchasing managers’ index (PMI), is a monthly indicator of U.S. economic activity based on a survey of purchasing managers at more than 300 manufacturing firms.

One cannot invest in an index.

Risks: All investments have risks. Mairs & Power Growth Fund is designed for long-term investors.

Equity investments are subject to market fluctuations and the Fund’s share price can fall because of weakness in the broad market, a particular industry or specific holdings. Investments in small and mid-cap companies generally are more volatile. International investing risks include among others political, social or economic instability, difficulty in predicting international trade patterns, taxation, and foreign trading practices and greater fluctuations in price than U.S. corporations.

This commentary includes forward-looking statements such as economic predictions and portfolio manager opinions. The statements are subject to change at any time based on market and other conditions. No predictions, forecasts, outlooks, expectations or beliefs are guaranteed.

Foreside Fund Services, LLC. is the Distributor for Mairs & Power Funds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Gabelli ABC Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
INVESTMENT OBJECTIVE

The Gabelli ABC Fund's (MUTF:GABCX) investment objective is to achieve total returns that are attractive to investors in various market conditions without excessive risk of capital loss. The Fund focuses on arbitrage strategies investing in event driven situations such as announced mergers, spin-offs, split-ups, liquidations, and reorganizations - and may hold a significant portion of its assets in U.S. Treasury bills in anticipation of quick, non-market correlated opportunities. The Fund may also invest in value-oriented common stocks and convertible securities.

FIRST QUARTER COMMENTARY

For the first quarter of 2025, globally announced mergers and acquisitions (M&A) activity totaled $885 billion, a 15% increase relative to the first quarter of 2024 and a 6% increase sequentially. Domestically, $386 billion worth of deals were agreed upon off the back of more than 2,700 deals. Dealmaking within Europe totaled $154 billion, increasing 12%, and within Asia Pacific totaled $187 billion, increasing 59%.

Technology, financials, and energy and power were the three most active sectors in the quarter, accounting for more than $456 billion in announced deal activity. Noteworthy deals announced included the acquisition of Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA) by Sycamore Partners in a private takeover, Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) acquisition of Intra-Cellular Therapies, and Australia-based James Hardie signing an agreement to acquire AZEK Co.

Private equity-backed transactions totaled $185 billion during the first three months, contributing 21% to total deal volumes for a 26% year-over-year increase. Financial buyers continue to retain record levels of dry powder, which is expected to be deployed over the coming years.

The first few months of President Trump's second term were bumpier than many merger arbitrage investors had expected. Regulatory scrutiny remained at the forefront, as two transactions were sued by the Department of Justice and Federal Trade Commission within the first few weeks following inauguration. However, this may not be entirely indicative of the stance under this administration, as there is a new chair heading up the antitrust division of the DOJ as well as the FTC, both selected by President Trump, who have said they will enforce the laws as they were written. Investors will continue to closely monitor the public stances of these two agencies and the impacts they may have on the appetite of future deal making.

Corporate boardrooms and private equity buyers will be focused on several key items over the coming months, including antitrust oversight, the geopolitical and economic environments, and attractiveness of valuations. The drivers for M&A activity to accelerate over the coming years remain in place and include the need to compete on a global basis, particularly with potential tariff impacts, acquire new technological advancements, and enter new and growing business units. We expect to see a robust level of new deal announcements in the months ahead as we gain further clarity on a number of items which have been recent headwinds.

Our top contributors to performance for the quarter (based upon price change and position size) were Lennar Corp. Class A (LEN, 20.6% of net assets as of March 31, 2025, +1.60%), Fox Corp. Class B (FOXF, 1.6%, +0.18%), Millrose Properties Inc. Class A (MRP, 2.5%,+0.07%), Wide Open West, Inc. (0.6%, +0.06%), and United States Cellular Corp. (USM, 0.6%, +0.05%). Our top detractors were Lennar Corp. Class B (LEN.B, -17.2%, -1.97%), Rogers Corp. (RCI, 0.4%, -0.08%), CNH Industrial NV (CNH, 0.7%, -0.06%), KKR & Co. Inc. (1.2%, -0.05%), and Venture Global, Inc. Class A (VG, 0.2%, -0.05%).

The Fund invests in announced mergers or acquisitions; the Fund is subject to the risk that the announced merger or acquisition may not be completed, may be negotiated at a less attractive price, or may not close on the expected date

DEALS IN THE PIPELINE

Beacon Roofing Supply Inc. (0.1% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (BECN - $123.70 - NASDAQ is a distributor of residential and non-residential roofing materials and other building products in the U.S. and Canada. After months of QXO Inc. seeking to negotiate with BECN, QXO went hostile with its offer to acquire the company for $124.25 cash per share. The parties finally agreed to terms at $124.35 on March 20, 2025, with the deal now expected to close by the end of April.

Frontier Communications Inc. (0.1%) (FYBR - $35.86 - NASDAQ) offers fiber optic networks to over 2.2 million subscribers across 25 states. On September 5, 2024, the company signed a definitive agreement to be acquired by Verizon Communications in which shareholders would receive $38.50 cash per share for a total deal value of $9.6 billion. The deal has received approval from shareholder, but remains subject to state utility regulatory clearances and is expected to close before the first quarter of 2026.

Logility Supply Chain Solutions Inc. (0.2%) (LGTY - $14.26 - NASDQ) provides supply chain management and enterprise software solutions. On January 24, 2025, the company signed a definitive agreement to be acquired by Aptean, in which shareholders would receive $14.30 cash per share. The deal has cleared all regulatory hurdles and is expected to close early in the second quarter, following shareholder approval.

CLOSED DEALS

Manitex International is a provider of mobile truck cranes, industrial lifting solutions, and aerial work platforms. On September 12, 2024, the company entered a definitive agreement to be acquired by Tadano for $5.80 cash per share. The parties closed the transaction on January 2, 2025, after receiving final regulatory approvals.

SecureWorks Corp. offers cybersecurity, automated detection, and response software. On October 21, 2024, the company signed an agreement to be acquired by Sophos for $8.50 cash per share. The deal had already received sign off from a majority of shareholders via written consent, and closed on February 3, 2025, after the remaining conditions were completed.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Lennar Corp. (LEN) 20.6%
Intra-Cellular Therapies Inc. 3.1
Millrose Properties Inc. (MRP) 2.5
Fox Corp. (FOXF) 1.6
TXNM Energy Inc. (TXNM) 1.4
KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) 1.2
Intevac Inc. 1.1
AllianceBernstein Holding LP (AB) 1.1
Juniper Networks Inc. (JNPR) 1.0
Tegna Inc. (TGNA) 1.0%
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS: Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (A)

data.title: Whitehaven Coal: Caught In The Political Crossfire
data.description:
With politics, actions speak far louder than words. So, investors need to concentrate on what is happening while learning to ignore the speech distractions until those speeches are turned into actions. Whitehaven Coal (OTCPK:WHITF) is a company that finds itself a major target of the current administration because it is an Australian coal company (reporting in Australian dollars unless otherwise noted) with major Japanese Steel customers. If the analysis only involved operations, everything would be fine. But there is a major United States political issue involving imported steel that could change the future of this company.

The political movement in the United States heavily favors reshoring jobs in the United States. However, that movement faces headwinds in the form of 500,000 manufacturing jobs that we already cannot fill. Reshoring more jobs means that this will only get worse.

The United States is already the number 2 manufacturing country in the world. The problem here is that Americans do not want the manufacturing jobs that would be reshored. President Trump is not the first president to run into this issue, as it goes at least as far back as President Obama. But that means that the deportation program is making the situation worse. Any reshoring of manufacturing jobs is going to have to be coordinated with the immigrant program. Right now, the two are at odds, which is not good for the whole steel tariff situation affecting the customers of Whitehaven Coal. It is also not good for the potential steel supply situation in the United States.

As a teacher, many of us are taught to educate the students about the effects of automation. The old days of factory jobs have been revolutionized through automation. In steel, for example, a lot of dangerous jobs that I audited are now gone to be replaced by machines that need trained technicians to monitor and manage. In fact, my first job has been automated also. Therefore, (for example), I tell students that want to go for welding that they need to also learn how to run and manage the welding machines being developed today that will likely replace a lot of welding jobs in their lifetime. But this often requires some college as well as technical training.

This is where education is now heading. The days of dropping out of school and getting a well-paying factory job are over. But we are not yet training the workforce to handle what industry needs.

As a result, a company like Whitehaven Coal may be in for a period of dislocation where there is a steel shortage, but there is no demand for its coal to make the steel as the politics and industry adjust to the automation that is occurring.

For the United States, the answer may well be the additional training needed to handle the automation underway. But it is clear now that the political solution as well as the educational solutions may lag.

What is not clear is what will happen to all the steelmakers that export steel to the United States.

The second issue is the advantage of natural gas over coal in generating electricity. But that means that coal is "taking it on the chin" for reasons that exist outside the industry.

Coal Demand

The interesting thing is that the demand for coal is still growing. We have yet to eliminate a major energy source. Even sources like coal are still growing, even though the mix of energy sources shows a decrease in the percentage generated from coal.

That is bullish for this company in the long term. But first the company has to get there.

Coal Prices

The company has set up guidance for coal prices. If that guidance continues to be met there should be no problem.

The issue, of course, is whether or not that guidance will hold up as shown above. While the United States actually has a fairly robust steel industry, it is not in the same form as it was when I had my first job at Bethlehem Steel (which is now gone).

Now there are a lot of companies that recycle used steel so that the demand for new steel has declined sharply from the good old days. A lot of "new" steel companies have arisen to fill the gap left by the demise of the previous steel manufacturing kings.

But these companies need a much more technical resources to run the machines that replaced workers doing frequently changing jobs.

This challenge is what may cause Whitehaven Coal some financial headaches until the market adjusts to the new reality (and politics as well). The last election saw a demand for some reconciliation with current pay levels to what is needed to buy a house (for example). That will not happen overnight, as the free market is a very messy place to handle transitions.

Last Article

The last article noted the current situation, which has now gotten worse with the implementation of tariffs on steel imports.

Shown below is an old presentation that I took from the last article because of its continuing relevance.

The problem with Whitehaven Coal is projecting the profitability of continuing coal use growth (which is very likely to happen). Unlike many things, the current administration is doing, imported steel has long been a major source of irritation for both political parties, all the way back to at least when I was working in the steel industry. Therefore, there could be a bit more political resolve on this issue, which may not bode well for Whitehaven Coal.

But this is far from the only view on the outcome. Therefore, I would tend to watch this from the sidelines because this type of dispute can be very dangerous to your wealth.

It is very much the opposite of what an investor needs to consider investing in an industry like coal. The last article noted that operations were doing fine, and contracts were honored.

While I think the chances of a major disruption are low, this is a very volatile issue and area for the United States.

There will be an annual update of results in August. Maybe by then we will know more about this situation. For me, right now, this is a sell, as I would not want to be caught in this situation despite the low stock price. For me, the situation is "too hot to handle" and it overrides, by a wide margin, current operating results and industry conditions.

There is always a possibility that the United States will increase its ability to recycle used steel. That would decrease the need for imported steel. But let us see the political solution first. The reality of needing years to adjust to a radical change seems to be "out the window".

Risk

The biggest risk here is that politics and reality seem to be far apart. A situation like that often costs a business big time somewhere in the supply chain. If, by some chance, sanity prevails, there will be probably time to evaluate this investment based upon the fundamentals. But right now, I view that situation as potentially too disruptive due to the sensitive nature of steel imports (compared to reshoring that industry).

Similarly, the company has been rearranging the portfolio to accommodate a changing industry environment. Now the threat of that strategic decision is that more changes may need to happen or even an industry shrinkage.

Probably the major result of the current situation will be a recession. But that risk is not good for the company either.

There are a lot of things here that can influence the future of the company. Yet management has no control over much of it.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Gabelli Utilities Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
THE GABELLI UTILITIES FUND

The Gabelli Utilities Fund (MUTF:GABUX) is a diversified fund whose investment objectives are long term growth of capital and income. The Fund invests in companies that provide products, services, or equipment for the generation or distribution of electricity, gas, and water. Additionally, the Fund will invest in companies in telecommunications services or infrastructure services.

In the first quarter of 2025, the S&P Utilities Index (SPU) returned 4.9% compared to the S&P 500 Composite return of -4.3%. Regulated utilities outperformed non-regulated power companies and the overall market, primarily due to their defensive characteristics. Utilities are insulated from potential tariff impacts while benefiting from lower interest rates, manufacturing onshoring, and increased energy demand. The median first quarter 2025 return of our universe of 50 purely regulated electric, gas, and water utilities was an even stronger 9.0%.

After two decades of flat growth, U.S. electric demand is set for its fastest growth since the 1950s to 1970s era. The mega-cap tech companies (hyperscalers) are aggressively courting electric utilities to build the infrastructure to power mega-data centers, some of which use as much power as small cities. In addition, ongoing electrification and manufacturing onshoring are adding to electric demand. Regulated electric utilities benefit from selling existing power capacity, adding additional power capacity (including batteries), and upgrading/ expanding the transmission and distribution network. Natural gas utility and midstream companies benefit from an expected increase in gas demand to power growth.

Politicians and public utility regulators support the investment for economic and tax base reasons, but also the greater demand helps overall customer affordability. The Trump administration has adopted a more economical approach to meeting electric demand, which means more gas-fired generation, nuclear development, and ongoing renewable development. We expect the relaxation of some environmental and regulatory rules to help infrastructure development. However, potential changes to the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) clean energy tax credits continue to concern investors in clean energy stocks. Utilities have shifted to expansion mode, driving higher capital investment, rate base growth, EPS growth, and consolidation.

Our near-term utility outlook is favorable, based on the potential for higher EPS growth amid a slowing economy and a lower yield curve. Finally, the value of existing infrastructure has become more attractive to potential buyers and consolidation activity has picked up.

Year-to-date, some of the Fund's top contributors were National Fuel Gas (NFG, 7.8%; 31.4%), American Electric Power (AEP, 3.9%; 19.6%), WEC Energy Group (WEC, 3.4%; 16.9%), and Ameren (AEE, 3.0%; 13.5%).

Portfolio detractors included Edison International (EIX, 0.9%; -25.4%), Cameco Corp. (CCJ, 0.7%; -19.9%), and AES Corp. (AES, 2.5%; -2.0%).

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS
NextEra Energy Inc. (NEE) 7.6%
National Fuel Gas Co. (NFG) 7.5
American Electric Power Co. Inc. (AEP) 3.9
Evergy Inc. (EVRG) 3.7
WEC Energy Group Inc. (WEC) 3.4
Southwest Gas Holdings Inc. (SWX) 3.2
ONEOK Inc. (OKE) 3.0
Ameren Corp. (AEE) 3.0
Eversource Energy (ES) 2.6
AES Corp. (AES) 2.4
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Evergy, Inc. (3.7% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (EVRG - $68.95 - NASDAQ), based in Kansas City, Missouri, is an electric and gas utility serving 1.6 million electric customers in central and eastern Kansas, including the cities of Topeka, Lawrence, Manhattan, Hutchinson, and Wichita and in western Missouri, including Kansas City. In mid-2018, Westar Energy and Great Plains Energy combined to form Evergy. EVRG owns a 15,400 MW generation portfolio (5,900 MWs of coal, 4,100 MW's gas, 4,400 MW's wind, 1,100 nuclear). EVRG expects to earn the top-end of its 4%-to-6% EPS CAGR (from 2025 EPS of $4.02). We believe the growth rate is conservative and will likely be raised. EVRG is methodically integrating strong potential economic load growth (11.2GW pipeline) into sales, rate base, and eventually EPS growth. EVRG's current forecast assumes 2%-3% load growth CAGR through 2029 based on 500-MW's of new load. The forecast does not include 1.6 GW's of load in the "Finalizing Agreements" stage of its economic development pipeline. The solidified 2026-2029 load growth is from 500-MW of the 800MW under active construction, including a $1 billion data center, due in 2028; a $4 billion manufacturing plant for Panasonic, which will produce 4000 new jobs, due in 2026; and an $800 million data center for Meta, due in 2027.

National Fuel Gas Co. (7.5%) (NFG - $79.19 - NYSE), based in Williamsville, New York, is a gas and pipeline utility with a growing exploration and production business. The gas utility serves 754,000 customers in Buffalo, New York, and Erie and Sharon, Pennsylvania. The pipeline & storage (P&S) business operates 3,000 miles of pipe and 34 storage facilities, primarily in the state of New York. The E&P business, Seneca Resources, operates in Appalachia (owns 1.2 million net acres), primarily the Marcellus and Utica shales. Seneca's proved gas reserves at year-end FY 2024 were 4,753 Bcfe (compared to 4,536 Bcfe in FY 2023). Forward gas prices have risen due to growing LNG exports and growing electric power demand. NFG is using free cash flow to buyback stock, improve credit profile, and overall financial strength. NFG raised the annual dividend for the 54th consecutive year to $2.06 per share from $1.98 per share.

NextEra Energy Inc.'s (7.6%) (NEE - $70.89 - NYSE) primary subsidiary, Florida Power & Light (FP&L), based in Juno Beach, Florida, is the largest electric utility in the state, and NextEra Energy Resources (NER), is the nation's leading renewable owner and operator. FP&L serves 5.8 million customers in Eastern, Southern, and Central-Western Florida (35 GWs of generation: 73% gas, 14% nuclear, 14% other). NER owns 34 GWs of net generation, including the nation's largest wind portfolio. NEE owns 101.4 million common units (~58%) of XPLR Infrastructure (XIFR-8.97-HOLD). NEE continues to expect to earn at or near the top of its 6-8% growth rate from 2025-2027.

Southwest Gas Holdings Inc. (3.2%) (SWX - $71.80 - NYSE) is a natural gas utility serving 2.1 million customers in geographically diverse portions of Arizona (1.132 million, or 53%), Nevada (790,000, or 37%), and California (201,000, or 10%). Centuri is an infrastructure utility services contractor specializing in underground gas utility piping, electric distribution, and 5G data. Icahn owns over 10% of shares and was influential in placing four board members on the company's eleven person board. On April 22, 2024, SWX issued 14.3 million shares of Centuri at $21 per share and privately-placed 2.6 million shares to Icahn. Proceeds of $329 million will be used to paydown Centuri debt. SWX owns 81% of the outstanding 88.5 million shares, valued at roughly $1.08 billion, or $15 per SWX share. SWX plans to divest its stake through various options, including a sale of CTRI shares, a distribution to SWX shareholders, a potential exchange of CTRI shares for SWX shares or some combination. SWX would then be a fully regulated gas utility operating in the fast growing states of Arizona and Nevada as well as in California.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS

data.title: ChargePoint: Weak Quarter And Disappointing Outlook - Sell (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. or "ChargePoint" (NYSE:CHPT), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier article on the company.

After the close of Wednesday's session, electric vehicle ("EV") charging solutions provider ChargePoint Holdings reported weaker-than-expected Q1/FY2026 results, with sales coming in below consensus estimates and substantial cash burn:

While higher-margin subscription revenues were up 14% on a year-over-year basis, sales of charging systems deteriorated to new multi-year lows:

On the conference call, management pointed to weakness in Germany as the primary reason behind the revenue shortfall.

On the flip side, consolidated non-GAAP gross margin of 31% reached a new multi-year high:

On the call, management attributed persistent improvement to increased margins in both hardware and subscription, as well as subscription sales growing as a percentage of total revenue.

ChargePoint does not anticipate tariffs to have a material impact on costs of goods sold. As a result, gross margins are expected to continue their upward trajectory later this year.

Disappointingly, operating expenses increased materially on a sequential basis which management attributed to "annual raises and investments in certain key areas of the business":

The combination of lower sales and higher operating expenses resulted in a sequential drop in Adjusted EBITDA:

While inventories were up slightly from the end of fiscal year 2025, the increase was solely a result of currency fluctuations. The company continues to expect inventory to decrease over the course of FY2026.

After burning $34 million in cash during the quarter, ChargePoint ended Q1 with $196 million in unrestricted cash and $300 million in debt. On the call, management pointed to a number of large annual payments as the primary reason for the substantial sequential increase in cash usage.

While the company retains access to its up to $150 million revolving credit facility, harsh minimum liquidity covenants effectively prevent ChargePoint from drawing under the facility.

Unfortunately, market conditions remain challenging as evidenced by the company's disappointing second quarter guidance with sales projected to be in a range of $90 million to $100 million, well below consensus expectations.

We are guiding with caution due to the continued changes in the macro environment, including tariff uncertainty as well as our near-term focus on operationalizing our partnership with Eaton.

While there is always a possibility of headwinds from deterioration in macro conditions, we expect revenue upside later in the year from the introduction of our new AC hardware that Rick outlined, better performance in Europe and growth from our new partnership with Eaton.

However, management still expects the company to achieve positive Adjusted EBITDA before the end of the current fiscal year.

Considering current market conditions, I do not expect the company to come even close to Adjusted EBITDA profitability this year.

In addition, substantial cash usage remains a cause for concern, particularly given the requirement to refinance $300 million of convertible notes ahead of their April 1, 2028 maturity date.

Clearly, my upgrade from "Sell" to "Hold" in early December following improved Q3/FY2025 results has been premature, as the stock has lost almost 50% of its value since that time:

Adding insult to injury, investors will likely have to prepare for a reverse stock split later this year in order to regain compliance with the New York Stock Exchange's continued listing standards (emphasis added by author).

On February 19, 2025, ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. was notified by the New York Stock Exchange that it is not in compliance with Section 802.01C of the NYSE Listed Company Manual because the average closing price of the Company's Common Stock was less than $1.00 over a consecutive 30 trading-day period. (...)

The Company intends to consider available alternatives, including but not limited to, a reverse stock split, subject to stockholder approval no later than at the Company's next annual meeting of stockholders, if necessary, to cure the stock price non-compliance.

The combination of a challenging operating environment, substantial cash usage, looming debt issues, ambitious near-term profitability targets and the likely requirement to conduct a reverse stock is causing me to downgrade ChargePoint Holdings' common stock back to "Sell".

Bottom Line

ChargePoint Holdings reported underwhelming first quarter results and provided disappointing Q2 guidance.

While management still targets Adjusted EBITDA profitability later this year, challenging market conditions are not likely to abate anytime soon.

In addition, persistent cash usage does not bode well for the company's ability to address its 2028 convertible notes.

Lastly, investors need to be wary of the likely requirement to conduct a reverse stock split later this year.

Considering the headwinds discussed above, I am downgrading my rating on ChargePoint Holdings' common shares back to "Sell" and apologize for a bad call in December.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Gabelli U.S. Treasury Money Market Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
SHAREHOLDER COMMENTARY

The U.S. bond market dealt with turbulence and uncertainty in the first quarter of 2025, shaped by changing expectations for interest rates, shifting economic data, and rising geopolitical tension, especially following the new administration's announcement of sweeping tariffs. Early in the quarter, Treasury yields stayed elevated as strong consumer spending and solid job growth supported the outlook for continued economic strength. That narrative began to shift in March, when the administration unveiled a 10% blanket tariff on all imports and added harsher penalties for key trade partners like China. The move raised fresh concerns about inflation, global trade disruptions, and slower growth.

In response, investors turned to U.S. Treasuries as a safe haven, causing yields to fall across the curve. The 2-year yield (US2Y), a key gauge of market expectations for Fed policy, dropped from just over 4.24% at the start of the year to 3.88% by the end of March. The 10-year yield (US10Y) followed suit, falling from 4.56% to about 4.21%. These moves reflected growing uncertainty about the economy's direction and increased speculation that the Federal Reserve may need to ease policy sooner than expected.

Throughout the quarter, the Fed held rates steady at 4.25% to 4.50%, while maintaining a wait-and-see approach. Policymakers acknowledged progress in bringing down inflation but emphasized the need for more evidence before moving toward rate cuts. In a sign of growing caution, the Fed also announced it would begin slowing the pace of balance sheet runoff in April, easing one of the more restrictive parts of its current policy stance.

Economic data continued to send mixed signals. March payrolls came in strong, adding 228,000 jobs, but the unemployment rate edged up to 4.2% and wage growth slowed to 3.8%. Inflation cooled slightly, with March's Consumer Price Index rising 2.4% year-over-year; still above the Fed's target but moving in the right direction. Meanwhile, consumer confidence weakened and business sentiment declined, especially among small businesses grappling with the uncertainty surrounding new trade policies.

The market's turning point came in late March with the tariff announcement. Equities fell, Treasury demand spiked, and rate expectations quickly shifted. Fed funds futures began to price in rate cuts as early as June, even as the Fed stuck to its cautious, data-driven messaging. Chair Powell reiterated the need for clarity and sustained signs of slowing inflation and growth before the Fed would act.

As the quarter closed, the bond market was caught between two possibilities: a soft landing, where inflation falls without a sharp slowdown in growth, or a recession, triggered by rising costs, tighter financial conditions, and trade-related shocks. While the economy is still on relatively solid footing, the path ahead is increasingly uncertain. Markets remain highly sensitive to incoming data and policy developments. For now, the bond market is signaling caution, watching closely to see whether the economy can glide toward stability or if it is heading for a more difficult landing.

MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION

During the first quarter of 2025, our investment strategy remained squarely focused on preserving liquidity. We closely monitored macroeconomic developments, Federal Reserve policy signals, and investor cash flow needs to guide positioning. Treasury bill yields held firm near the upper end of the Fed's target range, while government money market funds continued to attract steady inflows, highlighting investors' ongoing preference for safety and flexibility amid equity market volatility.

Expectations for potential rate cuts later this year contributed to a flatter front-end yield curve. In response to continued geopolitical uncertainty, softening economic data, and a growing outlook for monetary easing, we maintained a conservative stance. The portfolio remained actively allocated to 3 and 6-month Treasury bills, each with average yields of approximately 4.28%. Our barbell strategy, combining ultra-short maturities for immediate liquidity with slightly longer positions to capture relative value for longer, ensures the portfolio remains flexible, resilient, and well-positioned in a shifting rate environment.

Money market funds remained a key destination for capital, reflecting ongoing investor demand for safety, liquidity, and yield. As of March 31, 2025, total money market fund assets climbed to approximately $6.98 trillion, up from $6.85 trillion at year- end 2024. Government money market funds accounted for the vast majority of this growth, now totaling $5.70 trillion, while prime funds held approximately $1.05 trillion and tax-exempt funds stood at $180 billion. This allocation underscores continued investor preference for high-quality, low-volatility instruments amid ongoing uncertainty. The Gabelli U.S. Treasury MMF remains strategically positioned to navigate this environment. Our focus on short-term Treasury securities ensures that the portfolio is both liquid and resilient. We continue to actively manage maturities and reinvest cash flows in alignment with prevailing market conditions and anticipated Fed policy. As the Fed maintains a cautious, data-dependent stance, our barbell structure enables us to respond dynamically to changes in yield curve shape, monetary policy, and broader macro developments.

FUND PERFORMANCE

For the 3 months ending March 31, 2025 the Gabelli U.S. Treasury Money Market Fund produced a return of 4.37% annualized, with a 7-day yield of 4.23%. The Fund's investment objective is to provide current income while maintaining liquidity and a stable share price of $1.00. The Fund invests exclusively in U.S. Treasury securities. As such, it is considered one of the most conservative investment options offered within the Gabelli complex. Although the Fund invests in short-term U.S. Government securities, the amount of income that a shareholder may receive will be largely dependent on the current interest rate environment.

PORTFOLIO HIGHLIGHTS
Total Net Assets: $5.6 Billion
NAV (Class AAA): $1.00
Inception Date: 10/01/92
Expense Ratio: (A) 0.08%
(A) As of the current prospectus dated January 28, 2025.
SHARE CLASS SYMBOL
Class 1: GABXX
Class A: GBAXX
Class C: GBCXX
Although the Fund seeks to preserve the value of your investment at $1.00 per share, it cannot guarantee it will do so. An investment in the Fund is not insured or guaranteed by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation or any other government agency. The Fund's sponsor has no legal obligation to provide financial support to the Fund, and you should not expect that the sponsor will provide financial support to the Fund at any time. You could lose money by investing in the Fund.

Judith A. Raneri, Ronald S. Eaker
